implausible_knowledge: “Someone called in about a strange vehicle sitting on this block.”
error: As she reaches the door, Manuel knows someone will notice this vehicle.
erroneous_event: They turn into a clean, quiet block. No tents. No graffiti. Trash where it belongs. She has him stop at a sleeping townhouse. “Stay. Think of something if anyone asks.” She walks to the door, pulls something from her coat, and pauses. <error>As she reaches the door, Manuel knows someone will notice this vehicle.</error> A moment later, she goes in without knocking.
story:
Manuel watches the rain work in the dark. Another year older and nothing to show for it. The Uber sign burns in the corner of the windshield, and a Dominican flag hangs from the mirror. A fan of losing scratchers lies on the passenger seat like dead leaves. The book on the radio tells him to rise at five, make his bed, and remember that you are only a victim if you choose to do nothing. He nods along in agreement as he works on his scratchers.

Half past midnight. The plane should be down. He holds a whiteboard: AGNIS.

She finds him. The most ordinary woman he’s ever seen. Laced shoes, plain coat, big glasses, a blue scarf, and a small leather bag.

“Agnis?”

“Yes. Manuel?”

“Yes, ma’am.”

“Get the door.”

He opens the rear door. She slides in. He kills the radio and drives.

Silence. Some passengers fill a car with talk; she subtracts herself from it. He can’t even hear her breathing. In the mirror she’s like a blank seat.

Then the questions come, clipped and cool: How long has he been driving? Does he make good money? He admits to eight years behind the wheel. Before that, baseball. One game in the majors. An arm that snapped. A meeting with a doctor. A coach who said he wasn’t worth the money. The walk out with a sling and a bag.

“How much on your best night?” she asks. “A thousand,” he says. “I’ll give you a thousand cash to log out and drive me somewhere else.” She peels off five hundred and drops it in his lap. “Five hundred now. The rest when we’re done.” He logs out.

They turn into a clean, quiet block. No tents. No graffiti. Trash where it belongs. She has him stop at a sleeping townhouse. “Stay. Think of something if anyone asks.” She walks to the door, pulls something from her coat, and pauses. As she reaches the door, Manuel knows someone will notice this vehicle. A moment later, she goes in without knocking.

The rain erases the street. The house sleeps peacefully in the storm's shower. Suddenly, in an upstairs window, flashes: two quick, a pause, two again, a longer pause, then one final blink. The hairs on his neck rise.

He drops the car into drive when the Red and blue lights bloom behind him. Their color pours through the rain. Mierda. He sets it back in park and tries to breathe normally. He thinks to tell them he saw a murder. Then he thinks of papers he don’t have. Dios, perdoname. An officer knocks. “Evening. Officer Sidney Greene. Someone called in about a strange vehicle sitting on this block. License and registration, please.” Manuel can’t find words.

Then a voice behind the officer speaks. “Excuse me,” says the woman under an umbrella. Her face is blurry in its shadow. “Is there a problem? I have an early flight.” The officer looks from her to Manuel. “Are you her ride?” Manuel nods. The officer apologizes and leaves. She gets back in. “Drive,” she says, satisfied and tired.

She fills the car now, leaning into the middle, present and smiling. “In weather like this, more accidents happen than at any other time,” she muses. “Funny how the murders go down. Rain makes cameras useless. You would think more would take place.” He says he saw nothing. She has him pull under a dead streetlight. He clicks the dome light on. She keeps her eyes on him like a cat watching a mouse.

“What happened was fate,” she says. “Do you believe in fate? In luck? You play scratchers; you must.” A coin appears, rolls over her knuckles. She flips the coin, catches it, and covers it on the console. “Call it.”

He pleads not to. She presses. “If you call it right, you go free with your money. If you call it wrong, well… you’ve seen too much.” He whispers, “Heads.”

A slow inhale. Her hand lifts. He doesn’t look. Then he closes his eyes.

“Well done.” She laughs. “Give me your phone and keys.”

He doesn’t understand. He waits for a shot to the back of his head. She shakes him by the shoulder instead.

“Phone. Keys. Now. Give them to me.”

He hands them to her over his shoulder, not looking back. He hears her rustle in the back seat, then the opening of the rear door before it slams shut.

The dome light keeps humming. He forces his neck to turn and face where the coin lay, tails side up. His face twists and contorts, trying to make sense of what he saw. Heads. He had told her heads.

Two soft taps on his window. He closes his eyes and turns to meet whatever comes next.
